data.title: Warren calls for tariff refund for consumers after Supreme Court ruling
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) on Friday called for tariff refunds be sent to U.S. consumers after the Supreme Court struck down the bulk of President Trump’s sweeping emergency tariffs.

“No Supreme Court decision can undo the massive damage that Trump’s chaotic tariffs have caused,” Warren wrote on social platform X. “The American people paid for these tariffs and the American people should get their money back.”

In a 6-3 ruling, the justices overturned Trump’s expanded use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose tariffs on nearly every country. The president has argued that the import taxes are crucial to national security and suggested a ruling against them would be a “terrible blow” to the U.S. economy.

Trump, who called the decision a “disgrace” on Friday, was the first president to invoke the law to impose tariffs

How a potential refund could work remains unclear as it will be a battle held in the lower courts. The U.S. has garnered $289 billion in revenue from Trump’s tariff policy, according to the Bipartisan Policy Center. Hundreds of companies have already filed lawsuits seeking to protect their claims.

One of the case’s lead plaintiffs, founder of wine importer V.O.S. Selections Victor Schwartz, said he will push for a refund after “thousands of American small businesses like mine were thrown into chaos” following Trump’s enactment of his tariff policy last April on what he called “Liberation Day.” The impact of the levies was “unpredictable” and “bad business,” he added.

“The administration’s tariff taxes — which my business was forced to pay — threatened our survival,” Schwartz said in a statement.

Chief Justice John Roberts, who joined the majority with Justices Elena Kagan, Ketanji Brown Jackson, Sonia Sotomayor, Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett, wrote that the president “must ‘point to clear congressional authorization’ to justify his extraordinary assertion of that power. He cannot.”

Justice Brett Kavanaugh, who issued the dissent with fellow conservative Justices Clarence Thomas and Samuel Alito, said Thursday’s decision says nothing about how refunds will be doled out, describing that process as “likely to be a ‘mess.'”

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) praised the court’s decision, calling it a “win for the wallets of every American consumer.”

“Trump’s chaotic and illegal tariff tax made life more expensive and our economy more unstable,” Schumer said in a statement. “Families paid more. Small businesses and farmers got squeezed. Markets swung wildly.”

He added, “We’ve said from day one: a president cannot ignore Congress and unilaterally slap tariffs on Americans. That overreach failed.”

Former Solicitor General Neal Katyal, who served under the Obama administration, also praised the court’s decision invalidating the tariffs.

“This case has always been about the presidency, not any one president. It has always been about separation of powers, and not the politics of the moment,” he said in a statement.

“I’m gratified to see our Supreme Court, which has been the bedrock of our government for 250 years, protect our most fundamental values,” Katyal added.

data.title: GOP Sen. John Curtis praises Supreme Court ruling against Trump tariffs
data.description:
Utah Sen. John Curtis (R) commended the Supreme Court’s Friday ruling that President Trump unlawfully utilized an emergency statute to impose his administration’s sweeping tariffs. 

“Today’s ruling affirms, despite all the noise of the moment, that the Founders’ system of checks and balances remains strong nearly 250 years later,” Curtis wrote in a statement. 

The U.S. Constitution grants Congress authority over taxation. Tariffs are taxes on imported goods. 

The president has made tariffs a central component of his administration’s economic policies, using these policies as negotiation tactics to broker new agreements with the U.S.’s trading partners. 

The move has faced significant pushback from elected Democrats and some Republicans. Last October, the Senate passed a resolution to condemn these tariffs with the support of four Republican legislators, including GOP Sen. Rand Paul (Ky.), who sponsored the measure. 

The vote reflected a growing dissatisfaction among American business leaders and consumers with these tariffs. Despite the White House’s promises that foreign countries would eat the costs of the increased taxes, a recent report from the Kiel Institute for the World Economy found that U.S. consumers and importers are bearing 96 percent of costs resulting from these policies. 

Curtis was not among the Republican legislators who voted for a bipartisan resolution to condemn Trump’s tariffs and push for their repeal. The GOP legislator, who sits on the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation, said the Friday ruling leaves lingering issues to be resolved. 

“Several questions remain unanswered, including what happens to the revenue already collected and how the Administration may use alternative authorities to impose tariffs,” Curtis said in the statement. “Looking ahead, it is critical that we provide the clarity and predictability businesses need.”

The Supreme Court did not provide guidance to lower courts on how to navigate expected refund requests from companies hoping to recover billions of dollars they paid for these tariffs. Hundreds of companies, including Costco, sectors of the Toyota Group and Revlon, have already filed lawsuits in the lead up to this decision.

data.title: Victory and backing Israel aren't conflicting goals for Democrats — they're the same
data.description:
I had the honor of representing North Carolina’s 6th District in Congress — a district that flipped from red to blue in 2020 for the first time in more than three decades. It was a genuinely competitive congressional district where a Democrat like myself could win only by building a broad coalition and earning the trust of independents and moderates. In races like mine, reality is shaped by voters on the ground, not by the loudest voices online.

That’s why I know Democratic Majority for Israel PAC’s new 2026 Majority Project, announced this week, is spot on. Democratic Majority for Israel PAC is the only organization focused on two goals that Democrats cannot afford to separate: electing pro-Israel Democrats and winning back a Democratic majority in the House.

Control of the House majority will hinge on a small number of races. Democrats cannot afford to get this wrong. If we want to govern — to protect democracy, tackle affordability, lead responsibly on foreign policy, and serve as a check on President Trump — we must nominate Democrats who can win tough general elections and be effective once elected.

That is exactly what the Majority Project is designed to do. At its core, the project reflects a simple but often overlooked truth: winning the House for Democrats and supporting Israel as a democratic ally are not in conflict. They reinforce each other.

Americans can hold two truths at once. Hamas’s Oct. 7 attack and hostage-taking were moral atrocities. And the suffering of civilians in the war is real and demands sustained humanitarian attention. Democrats should speak honestly about both — with compassion and moral clarity.

That same moral clarity also means being honest about the endgame. With the hostages returned and a fragile ceasefire in place, we must relentlessly pursue a path that guarantees Israel’s security, ends Hamas’s reign of terror and ushers in a brighter future for Israelis and Palestinians.

But it is politically and strategically reckless to pretend that support for Israel is outdated or optional. Mainstream Democrats understand that the U.S.–Israel alliance strengthens our national security while delivering innovations that make Americans healthier, safer and more productive. I’ve seen it firsthand in a purple state with military bases, world-class hospitals and universities, high-tech companies, and farmers who all benefit directly from American-Israeli partnerships. Supporting this relationship tangibly improves people’s lives at home.

Our polling and data continues to show that most Democratic and swing voters believe Israel has a right to exist as a Jewish homeland and want the U.S. to maintain a strong alliance with Israel.

Competitive districts are decided at the margins. If the party alienates moderates, independents or persuadable Democrats, we lose. And when Democrats lose competitive seats, we don’t just lose votes — we lose the majority. We lose the gavel. We lose committee chairs. We lose the ability to pass legislation, protect democratic norms and deliver for the American people.

That’s why the Democratic Majority for Israel PAC’s Majority Project matters. These endorsements reflect a clear, disciplined strategy: support Democrats who hold mainstream, pro-Israel views of American foreign policy and who are best positioned to win their races.

In safe Democratic seats, this means supporting candidates aligned with the vast majority of Democratic voters — those who back Israel as a democratic ally and view the U.S.–Israel relationship as a core pillar of American foreign policy — and pushing back on candidates with radical anti-Israel views.

The truth is a handful of high-profile primaries can create a distorted picture of the party, especially when social media amplifies the most absolutist positions. That may generate attention, but it is not a governing strategy. Results in a few cherry-picked primaries should not be mistaken for the broader Democratic electorate — and they certainly should not be confused with what it takes to win swing districts where control of the House will be decided.

The reality is that many voters hold two views at the same time: they can be critical of specific decisions or strategies set by the Israeli government, and they can still support Israel’s right to exist and the importance of the U.S.–Israel relationship. Candidates who treat those fundamentals with contempt don’t broaden the tent — they shrink it. 

In the weeks ahead, our PAC will announce additional endorsements and investments to ensure pro-Israel Democrats have the resources to compete and win the majority. 

I’ve lived the reality of competitive races. I know how narrow the path to victory can be. And I know this much: Democrats are strongest when our values match our governing goals — protecting democracy, standing with allies like Israel, opposing terrorists like Hamas, and staying engaged in the hard work of diplomacy so our leadership doesn’t disappear when it’s most needed.

Kathy Manning chairs the Democratic Majority for Israel board. She was the first woman to serve as board chair of the Jewish Federations of North America.

data.title: Trump loosens restrictions on toxic pollution, including mercury and lead, from coal plants
data.description:
The Trump administration on Friday moved to loosen restrictions on toxic power plant pollution, including releases of neurotoxins mercury and lead.

On Friday, it revoked portions of Biden-era standards that tightened restrictions on how much mercury, lead and arsenic these plants can release.

The move is one in a series of steps from the Trump administration to reduce environmental standards in order to bolster fossil fuels.

The Biden-era standards in question, which President Trump is proposing to rescind, would have upped mercury controls by 70 percent for a subset of power plants that use a type of coal known as lignite. The Trump rule, which would reinstate 2012 standards, would have these plants operating under looser mercury limits than other coal plants.

The Biden-era standards also would have sought to reduce emissions from all coal plants of lead, nickel and arsenic by 67 percent.

Mercury is a neurotoxin. Exposure to it can cause harm to a person’s brain, heart, kidneys, lungs and immune system. Lead is also a neurotoxin, especially in children. Arsenic exposure can cause cancer.

The Trump administration argued in a Friday press release that it was getting rid of moves that hampered the coal industry. It also estimated that it would save Americans a total of $670 million.

“The Biden-Harris Administration’s anti-coal regulations sought to regulate out of existence this vital sector of our energy economy. If implemented, these actions would have destroyed reliable American energy,” said EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin in a written statement. 

The move reverts back to 2012 standards that were issued by the Obama administration.

data.title: Watch live: Trump gives remarks after Supreme Court strikes down tariffs
data.description:
President Trump will speak with reporters Friday after the Supreme Court struck down nearly all of the administration’s sweeping tariffs on foreign trading partners.

The high court rejected Trump’s expanded use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to impose the steep import taxes. The 1970s-era law allows the president to “regulate” imports when necessary to respond to national emergencies that pose an “unusual and extraordinary” threat.   

During a breakfast with governors earlier Friday, Trump called the decision a “disgrace.”

Justices Samuel Alito, Clarence Thomas and Brett Kavanaugh dissented. Conservative Justices Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett and Chief Justice John Roberts sided with the majority.

The press conference is scheduled to begin at 12:45 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Trump tells governors Supreme Court tariff decision is a 'disgrace'
data.description:
President Trump called the Supreme Court’s decision striking down a bulk of his tariffs imposed under emergency powers “a disgrace” during a private meeting with governors on Friday, a source familiar told The Hill.

The court’s decision came just as Trump was hosting governors for a breakfast at the White House. He is expected to brief reporters this afternoon from the press briefing room, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on the social platform X.

The decision came down from the high court during the meeting, marking a significant defeat for Trump, who has made tariffs a centerpiece of his economic agenda.

In a 6-3 ruling, the justices rejected Trump’s expanded use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) in imposing tariffs on nearly every country. The 1970s law allows the president to “regulate” imports when necessary to respond to national emergencies that pose an “unusual and extraordinary” threat.  

“We claim no special competence in matters of economics or foreign affairs,” Chief Justice John Roberts wrote.

“We claim only, as we must, the limited role assigned to us by Article III of the Constitution. Fulfilling that role, we hold that IEEPA does not authorize the President to impose tariffs.”

Two of Trump’s Supreme Court nominees, Justices Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett, ruled with Roberts alongside the court’s liberals against the president’s tariff policy.

Trump is the first president to invoke the IEEPA to impose tariffs.

The president’s political opponents were quick to celebrate the decision.

“This is a win for the wallets of every American consumer,” Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) said in a statement. “Trump’s chaotic and illegal tariff tax made life more expensive and our economy more unstable. Families paid more. Small businesses and farmers got squeezed. Markets swung wildly.”

Rep. Rand Paul (R-Ky.), a vocal GOP critic of Trump’s, applauded the ruling in a post on X.

“In defense of our Republic, the Supreme Court struck down using emergency powers to enact taxes,” Paul wrote. “This ruling will also prevent a future President such as AOC from using emergency powers to  enact socialism.”

White House chief of protocol Monica Crowley, meanwhile, said Trump has “plenty of tools in the tariff toolbox,” suggesting the president may seek other ways to impose the global duties.

“Don’t be a Panican,” Crowley wrote. “Trust Trump.”

data.title: Virginia judge blocks Democrats' redistricting referendum
data.description:
A Virginia judge dealt another blow on Thursday to Democrats’ redistricting efforts, blocking an April 21 referendum while the case is heard in court. 

Tazewell County Circuit Judge Jack Hurley Jr. granted a motion from the Republican National Committee that sought to block the April referendum, arguing against the timing and phrasing of the ballot question that would be posed to voters.

The emergency injunction, as shared by the Virginia Mercury, is in effect until March 18, though early voting on the referendum is scheduled to start March 6.

It’s the latest legal obstacle to Democrats’ redistricting plan in Virginia, where Democrats hold six of the state’s 11 congressional districts. Their plans for a redrawn map could give them as many as four pickup opportunities ahead of the high-stakes midterms. 

State lawmakers’ plan is to put the state constitutional amendment before voters in an April 21 referendum. If approved, the new map would temporarily take effect mid-decade, bypassing the state’s redistricting commission. 

Virginia is one of the latest states to wade into the national redistricting war as parties fight for control of Congress in the midterms. 

Republicans’ House campaign arm spokesperson Mike Marinella called the new ruling “a massive win.”

But Virginia Attorney General Jay Jones (D) said his office plans to “immediately appeal” the ruling, noting that these arguments are already before “the proper forum” in the Virginia Supreme Court.

Hurley had ruled earlier this month against the referendum in a separate matter, but the Supreme Court of Virginia this month allowed it to move forward as litigation proceeds.

data.title: Live updates: Supreme Court strikes down most of Trump's tariffs in 6-3 ruling
data.description:
The Supreme Court dealt a crushing blow to President Trump’s tariff agenda, ruling on Friday that his tariff scheme is illegal without congressional authorization. 

In one of the biggest cases justices have decided in years, the majority conservative court issued a rare rebuke of the president’s actions. Chief Justice John Roberts and Justices Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett — the latter two appointed by Trump — joining the three liberal justices in striking down the levies that were based on an international economic emergency.

Trump will hold a press briefing on the ruling at 12:45 p.m.

Meanwhile, Trump hosted governors at the White House for breakfast Friday, but not under the umbrella of the National Governors Association (NGA), whose meeting is underway in Washington. Trump has tangled with two Democratic governors — Wes Moore of Maryland and Jared Polis of Colorado — which cast a shadow on the breakfast and Saturday night’s annual dinner. Both decided to attend the meeting, reversing course.

Follow live below.

data.title: Hegseth criticizes 'Godless left' at Christian media convention
data.description:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth criticized “Godless” left and foreign ideologies while defending “Western Christian” ideas and traditions Thursday night at a Christian communicators conference in Nashville, Tenn.

“Gone is Godless and divisive [diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI)], gone is gender-bending equity and quotas, gone is climate change worship to a false God. We are one military, one fighting force, one nation under God. We are not in woke we trust, we are in God we trust,” Hegseth said as the headline speaker of the National Religious Broadcasters 2026 International Christian Media Convention.

Hegseth used his speech to rail against what he says is “a world beset by dangerous and godless foreign ideologies that sow doubt, sin, confusion and death,” while praising the agenda of President Trump.

The Trump administration last year banned tansgender service members from the military, cut numerous climate change and DEI-related programs and sought to curtail paying for military personnel to travel outside their state to seek an abortion.

“Under the leadership of President Trump, the military once again supports and trains our troops and tends to their spiritual health. You see, we train our troops, we no longer trans our troops,” Hegseth said.

Since taking the helm of the Pentagon last year, Hegseth has upended the tradition that defense secretaries stay out of public political commentary.

He has focused instead on shaping the military’s culture in more traditional Christian conservative views, enacting policy that bans transgender troops, reviews whether women should serve in combat roles and reevaluates the Defense Department’s support for Scouting America unless it institutes “core value reforms.”  

On Thursday, he insisted “the other side is fueled by godless and toxic ideologies, foreign to the western way of life, with tolerant hearts filled with rage and hate.”

Hegseth’s address directly follows his invitation to controversial Christian nationalist pastor Doug Wilson to give a sermon at the Pentagon on Tuesday.

Wilson led a worship service in the building as part of the military’s monthly prayer meeting, begun by Hegseth back in May. 

The Pentagon chief is the Trump administration’s most prominent follower of Wilson’s, whose church — the Communion of Reformed Evangelical Churches — preaches beliefs that homosexuality should be a crime, women should submit to their husbands and the 19th Amendment granting women the right to vote should be repealed.

Hegseth referred to the monthly prayer services in his speech, claiming the left “hate it.” 

“The left wing shrieks which means we’re right over the target,” he said to applause.

The convention also included a rendition of the National Anthem sung by Alexis Wilkins, the girlfriend of FBI Director Kash Patel, and a ceremonial color guard from the 101st Airborne Division’s band, the NRB said in a press release. 

The inclusion of the band is unusual as ceremonial support by the military typically shies away from religious services.

data.title: Les Wexner's attorney whispers 'I'll f—ing kill you' for giving long answers during deposition
data.description:
Billionaire retail mogul Les Wexner’s lawyer was caught on microphone threatening to “kill” Wexner for giving long answers during a Wednesday deposition related to his involvement with sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. 

“I will f—ing kill you if you answer another question with more than five words, okay,” Wexner’s lawyer said four and a half hours into the deposition with the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee on Wednesday.

Both Wexner and his attorney briefly chuckled, Wexner appearing to stifle his laughter as the attorney told him to “answer the question.” Wexner replied “okay” and the deposition proceeded. 

Wexner, who is connected to brands like Bath & Body Works, Abercrombie & Fitch and Victoria’s Secret, has long been criticized for being connected to Epstein, who served as his financial adviser from the 1990s into the early 2000s.

Virginia Giuffre, an outspoken victim of Epstein’s who died by suicide last year, reported being trafficked to Wexner, a claim he has denied. Files made public by the Department of Justice, show Wexner as a co-conspirator in the Epstein case on a 2019 FBI document though Wexner has not been charged with any crime. 

During Wednesday’s deposition, Wexner denied having any firsthand knowledge of Epstein’s sex trafficking operation. He told the committee that Epstein lived a “double life” and conned him and his wife and stole money from their family. Upon Epstein’s guilty plea and learning of his “deception” of their family, Wexner said he cut all ties with the convicted sex offender and did not visit him while he was in jail. 

“Let me state from the start: I was naive, foolish, and gullible to put any trust in Jeffrey Epstein. He was a con man. And while I was conned, I have done nothing wrong and have nothing to hide,” he said during his opening statement at the deposition. 

“Let me be crystal clear: I never witnessed nor had any knowledge of Epstein’s criminal activity,” Wexner added.
